headline: The 2026 MillerLite® BeerLeagueBaseball Draft Review: Where Bobby Witt Jr. Went First and Chaos Reigned Supreme
subheadline: Fourteen teams, 210 picks, one defending champion hunting back-to-back glory, and at least three managers who clearly drafted while operating heavy machinery.
body:
**I. The War Room Opens**

Every fantasy baseball draft has a moment — that crystallizing instant when you realize someone in your league has either lost their mind or achieved enlightenment, and you're not sure which. This year's MillerLite® BeerLeagueBaseball draft delivered that moment approximately forty-seven times. We're talking about a draft where the defending champion **Sugar Land Skeeters** sat at pick eleven like Michael Corleone at the baptism, calmly selecting **Fernando Tatis Jr.** while their competitors panicked around them. Where **North Correa** — a team that has finished 14th and 12th the last two years, the fantasy equivalent of the 2011-2024 White Sox — somehow landed the first overall pick and proceeded to draft like a man who just discovered the Player Rater exists. Where **Riverside Roughnecks** pulled off the heist of the century by letting **Gunnar Henderson** fall to pick seventeen, which is like finding a Rolex at a garage sale for eight dollars.

(Quick aside: This is my fifteenth year covering this league, and I've never seen a draft where the gap between best and worst picks was this wide. It's like watching *Succession* — everyone thinks they're Logan Roy, but most of them are actually Greg.)

The biggest steals? **Henderson** at 17, **Roki Sasaki** at 96, and **Luis Robert Jr.** at 141 for a team that desperately needed the upside swing. The worst reaches? We'll get there, but let's just say someone took **Mike Trout** in Round 6 like it was 2019 and his body wasn't being held together by prayers and surgical screws. The surprise moves? **BaseballPro77**, a former champion who's been steadily declining (7th last year, 2nd in 2024, champion in 2023), grabbed **Emmanuel Clase** in Round 4 like they're building their entire identity around holds and saves. Bold strategy, Cotton.

**II. Draft Order & Early-Pick Breakdown**

The draft order this year was determined by reverse standings, which meant **North Correa** (14th in 2024, 12th in 2025) got the 1-1 pick and the chance to reset their entire franchise. They took **Bobby Witt Jr.**, which was the correct choice — Witt slashed .332/.389/.556 with 32 homers and 31 steals in 2025, the kind of 5-tool production that makes you weep with joy in a 12-category league. No controversy there. The controversy is everything that came after.

**Athletic Park Dragons** went Ohtani at two (the batter-only version, which still delivered 49 homers and a 180 wRC+ because he's a literal video game character). **Wisconsin Rapids Reapers** took **Mookie Betts** third, which feels conservative but defensible given Mookie's 6.8 WAR and the position flexibility. **Abbotsford-Colby Twins** — your 2024 champions, still frisky — grabbed **Aaron Judge** at four, banking on another 50-homer season from a man whose body defies physics. And then things got interesting: **BaseballPro77** took **Elly De La Cruz** at five, betting on the 40-steal, 25-homer ceiling despite the .298 OBP that makes your eyes twitch. High risk, high reward. Very on-brand for a former champion trying to recapture glory.

**III. The Draft Grades: All 14 Teams**

**North Correa (Draft Pick #1)**
Look, I want to root for the underdog. I really do. **North Correa** has been the league's doormat for two consecutive years, finishing 14th and 12th with the kind of rosters that make you question whether they're even watching baseball. The good news? **Bobby Witt Jr.** at 1-1 was perfect — .332/.389/.556, 32 HR, 31 SB, 7.2 WAR. Untouchable. **Pete Alonso** at 28 gives them power (34 HR, 88 RBI in 2025). **Logan Gilbert** at 29 is a sneaky-good SP2 (3.41 ERA, 1.08 WHIP, 187 K). But then they took **Sandy Alcantara** in Round 8 and **Spencer Strider** in Round 9 — two pitchers coming off major injuries who might combine for 150 innings total. They're betting on ghosts. **Luis Robert Jr.** at 141 is the lottery ticket that could pay off (when healthy, he's a .280 hitter with 30-homer power), and **Dylan Crews** at 168 is pure upside. This is either a redemption arc or another year in the basement. **Grade: B-** — They nailed Round 1 and took too many injury risks after that.

**Athletic Park Dragons (Draft Pick #2)**
The Dragons went from 6th in 2024 to dead last in 2025, which is the fantasy equivalent of the 2023-2024 Diamondbacks in reverse. Their response? Bet the farm on pitching. **Shohei Ohtani** at two is unchallengeable (49 HR, 180 wRC+, alien status confirmed). **Matt Olson** at 27 is fine if uninspiring (28 HR, but that .227 average hurts in H/AB). But then they went **Kyle Schwarber** (36 HR, .197 AVG — ouch), **Michael King** (2.95 ERA, 201 K), **Shota Imanaga** (2.91 ERA, 3.01 FIP), **Aaron Nola** (3.57 ERA), and **Luis Castillo** (3.64 ERA). That's FIVE starting pitchers in their first seven rounds. In a 12-category league where you need to win IP, K, ERA, WHIP, and QS, this makes sense. But they're paper-thin on hitting. **Ezequiel Tovar** at 139 is a steal (finally figured it out at Coors, .285/.340/.476), but **Nick Castellanos** at 142 is a reach for a guy who posted a .306 OBP. **Grade: B** — Love the pitching depth, worried about everything else.

**Wisconsin Rapids Reapers (Draft Pick #3)**
A first-year team in 2025 that finished 10th, which is actually respectable. They're building smart. **Mookie Betts** at three is the safest pick possible — 6.8 WAR, .307/.399/.514, 22 HR, 13 SB, elite OBP. **Ketel Marte** at 26 gives them another .300 hitter with 20-homer pop. **William Contreras** at 31 is the best catcher after Adley, and getting him in Round 3 is excellent value. But then things get weird. They took FIVE relievers in the last six rounds (**Justin Martinez**, **Jeff Hoffman**, **Jason Foley**, **Jason Adam** — plus **Merrill Kelly** as their only SP after Round 7). That's a lot of faith in holds and saves. **Bryan Reynolds** at 59 is solid but unspectacular (.275, 24 HR), and **Christian Yelich** at 82 is a major injury risk. **Grade: B-** — Great top of draft, confusing bullpen hoarding.

**Abbotsford-Colby Twins (Draft Pick #4)**
The 2024 champions. Winners of a title. The team everyone wants to beat. They finished 6th last year, which suggests regression to the mean, but this draft screams 'we're still here.' **Aaron Judge** at four is obvious (53 HR, 144 RBI, 8.1 WAR in 2025 — the man is a golden god when healthy). **Jazz Chisholm Jr.** at 25 gives them speed and position flexibility. **Austin Riley** at 32 is the 30-homer third baseman you need. **Pablo López** at 53 is a steal — 3.32 ERA, 3.21 FIP, 198 K, one of the most underrated pitchers in baseball. **Spencer Schwellenbach** at 81 is a dart throw on a rookie who looked electric late in 2025 (2.48 ERA in his last 10 starts). This is a championship roster. **Kevin Gausman** at 137 is deep SP value, and **Xavier Edwards** at 116 is the sleeper speedster (.314/.382/.386, 42 SB in MiLB). **Grade: A-** — Dynasty energy, minimal reaches.

**BaseballPro77 (Draft Pick #5)**
The former champion (2023) has been sliding — 1st, 2nd, 7th over three years. That's the *Mad Men* trajectory, and they're currently in the 'Don Draper drinking alone in his apartment' phase. **Elly De La Cruz** at five is a high-variance pick — 26 HR, 67 SB, but that .298 OBP kills you in category leagues. **Jackson Merrill** at 24 is fantastic value (ROY finalist, .292/.323/.500, 24 HR). **Cole Ragans** at 33 is a great SP2 (3.14 ERA, 223 K). But **Emmanuel Clase** in Round 4? At pick 52? For a RELIEVER? In a league where you can find RP value late? This is either genius or madness. **Junior Caminero** at 89 is upside (top prospect, but .237 AVG in 2025), and **Bo Bichette** at 145 is a fall from grace (injured all of 2025, but the name value is still there). **Grade: B** — High ceiling, but the Clase pick feels like a head-scratcher.

**Albuquerque Bombers (Draft Pick #6)**
Finished 5th, 10th, 3rd the last three years. They're good, they're just not quite good enough. **José Ramírez** at six is the second-safest pick after Mookie — .285/.360/.538, 36 HR, 20 SB, iron-man durability. **Freddie Freeman** at 23 is aging gracefully (.310/.400/.520, 27 HR, walks a ton). **Garrett Crochet** at 34 is the breakout SP of 2025 (2.29 ERA, 209 K in 146 IP before the Red Sox acquisition). **Teoscar Hernández** at 51 gives them right-handed power. This is a well-constructed roster. **Logan Webb** at 62 is steady (3.47 ERA, 178 IP), **Willy Adames** at 79 is position flexibility, and **Colton Cowser** at 135 is the kind of late-round outfielder who could hit 25 homers. **Jasson Domínguez** at 146 is pure upside — if he figures it out, he's a top-50 player. **Grade: A-** — Balanced, experienced, ready to contend.

**Hiroshima Radiation (Draft Pick #7)**
The rollercoaster team: 2nd, 11th, 5th. They're either great or terrible, never boring. **Juan Soto** at seven is immaculate — .304/.426/.569, 41 HR, the best eye in baseball. **Trea Turner** at 22 is a bounceback candidate (.260/.328/.479 in an injury-shortened season, but still 15 SB in 80 games). **Blake Snell** at 35 is vintage Snell — 2.25 ERA in 128 IP, but with 'will he throw 160 innings?' concerns. **Jose Altuve** at 50 is the veteran you want (still hitting .285 at age 35). **Tyler Glasnow** at 63 is elite when healthy (2.61 ERA, 218 K, but the 'when healthy' caveat does a lot of work). This roster has All-Star talent at every level. **Anthony Santander** at 78 is power (44 HR in 2025 between Baltimore and Toronto). **Grade: A** — If the pitchers stay healthy, this is a title contender.

**Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs (Draft Pick #8)**
The model of consistency: 4th, 5th, 2nd the last three years. Always competitive, never champions. **Vladimir Guerrero Jr.** at eight is the pivot point of his career — .313/.401/.524, 35 HR, finally living up to the hype. **Bryce Harper** at 21 is still Bryce Harper (.290/.390/.545, 30 HR, MVP-level when right). **Corbin Burnes** at 36 gives them ace production (3.14 ERA, 195 K, horse innings). **Framber Valdez** at 49 is the sneaky-good SP2 (2.92 ERA, 198 IP, elite groundball rate). **Ronald Acuña Jr.** at 64 is THE boom-or-bust pick — coming off the torn ACL, he's either back to MVP form or damaged goods. **George Kirby** at 133 is outstanding late-round value (3.21 ERA, 189 K, innings eater). **Grade: A** — This is the roster that should've won already.

**Buttville Skidmarks (Draft Pick #9)**
Finished 8th two years running. The definition of mediocrity. **Kyle Tucker** at nine is elite (.302/.399/.566, 34 HR, 20 SB — five-category monster). **Zack Wheeler** at 20 is one of the best pitchers in baseball (2.57 ERA, 2.98 FIP, 213 K). **Rafael Devers** at 37 is power with a pulse (.289/.369/.578, 36 HR). **Corey Seager** at 48 is the veteran SS you want. But then they took **Jacob deGrom** at 65. JACOB DEGROM. The man has thrown 74 innings combined over the last two seasons. This is a reach wrapped in nostalgia wrapped in desperation. **Raisel Iglesias** at 76 is solid RP value, but **Nolan Arenado** at 205 is a reach for a guy who posted a 91 wRC+ last year. **Grade: B-** — Tucker and Wheeler are elite, but the deGrom gamble is dangerous.

**General Hospital (Draft Pick #10)**
The league's hospital ward: 13th, 13th the last two years. They're appropriately named. **Francisco Lindor** at ten is consistent greatness (.271/.343/.486, 33 HR, 29 SB). **Jarren Duran** at 19 is the breakout star (.293/.349/.501, 21 HR, 36 SB — legitimately elite). **Chris Sale** at 38 is the Cy Young who proved everyone wrong (2.38 ERA, 225 K, absolute monster season). **Brent Rooker** at 47 gives them power (39 HR, but playing time concerns with the Athletics). But then... **Mike Trout** at 75. Pick 75. For a guy who's played 130 games TOTAL since 2021. This is hope triumphing over evidence. This is drafting the 2012 version of a player who no longer exists. **Steven Kwan** at 122 is value (.319 AVG, elite contact), but **Michael Toglia** at 159 is a reach for a guy with a .220 average. **Grade: C+** — Great top three, then a Trout-sized crater in the middle.

**Sugar Land Skeeters (Draft Pick #11)**
The DEFENDING CHAMPIONS. Back-to-back is the goal. **Fernando Tatis Jr.** at eleven is exactly the kind of high-upside play a champion makes — .282/.346/.555, 33 HR, 24 SB, when he's right, he's transcendent. **Tarik Skubal** at 18 is the best pitcher in the draft (1.91 ERA, 0.91 WHIP, 228 K, absolute Cy Young dominance). **Cody Bellinger** at 39 is a nice rebound candidate (.265/.340/.478, 26 HR). **Yoshinobu Yamamoto** at 46 is electric (3.02 ERA, 174 K in 152 IP). **Brenton Doyle** at 67 is underrated (26 HR, 23 SB, finally hitting at Coors). **Roki Sasaki** at 95 is the STEAL OF THE DRAFT — the Japanese phenom, 102 mph heat, and he fell to Round 7 because of workload concerns. **Anthony Volpe** at 123 is MI depth, **Alec Bohm** at 130 is undervalued power. **Grade: A** — Champions draft like champions.

**Riverside Roughnecks (Draft Pick #12)**
Perennially mediocre: 9th, 9th, 11th. Stuck in the muck. But this draft might change everything. **Jackson Chourio** at twelve is the 20-year-old phenom (.275/.323/.468, 21 HR, 22 SB). And then they pulled off THE HEIST: **Gunnar Henderson** at 17. GUNNAR HENDERSON. The 23-year-old who hit .295/.375/.589 with 37 HR and 26 SB. He was ADP 6-8 in most leagues, and he fell to SEVENTEEN. (Someone tell me how this happened. I genuinely don't understand.) **Ozzie Albies** at 40, **Manny Machado** at 45, **James Wood** at 68 — this is a murderer's row of bats. **Roki Sasaki** went to the Skeeters, but **Bryce Miller** at 101 is excellent SP depth (3.14 ERA). **Austin Wells** at 157 is the sleeper catcher (20 HR as a rookie). **Grade: A+** — Henderson alone makes this an A, and they kept building.

**Canberra Wombats (Draft Pick #13)**
The inconsistent Aussies: 3rd, 12th, 4th. Boom or bust personified. **Corbin Carroll** at thirteen is the gamble — he slumped hard in 2025 (.227/.314/.387), but his 2023 ROY season showed what's possible. **Yordan Alvarez** at 16 is steady destruction (.296/.404/.579, 36 HR). **Wyatt Langford** at 41 is rookie upside. **CJ Abrams** at 44 is speed incarnate (31 SB, but the average is shaky). **Josh Hader** at 69 gives them elite closing. **Cal Raleigh** at 72 is the power-hitting catcher (31 HR). **Mark Vientos** at 97 is a breakout candidate (.285/.345/.512, 27 HR). **Shane McClanahan** at 181 is an injury risk but the talent is top-20 SP material. **Grade: B+** — If Carroll bounces back, this is an A.

**Benchwarmers (Draft Pick #14)**
Picking last sounds bad until you remember you also pick 15th. **Julio Rodríguez** at fourteen is the high-upside bet — he struggled in 2025 (.254/.313/.416) but the tools are still elite. **Paul Skenes** at 15 is the rookie sensation (1.96 ERA in 133 IP, 170 K, Rookie of the Year, future Cy Young candidate). That's a 1-2 punch with top-10 upside. **Oneil Cruz** at 42 is power and speed (25 HR, 25 SB, but the strikeouts). **Dylan Cease** at 43 gives them SP depth. **Adley Rutschman** at 71 is the best catcher in baseball when healthy (.276/.367/.452). **Tanner Bibee** at 99 is solid (3.54 ERA, good K rate). The end of the roster gets thin, but **Pete Crow-Armstrong** at 155 is pure upside. **Grade: B+** — Skenes and Julio could both be top-5 players if things break right.

**IV. Draft Day Heroes & Villains**

**THE 3 BEST VALUE PICKS:**

1. **Gunnar Henderson, Round 2, Pick 17 (Riverside Roughnecks)** — In what universe does a 23-year-old with 37 HR, 26 SB, and a .964 OPS fall out of the top 10? Henderson was consensus top-8 ADP. Getting him at 17 is like finding a Picasso at a Goodwill. This is theft. Someone should call the authorities.

2. **Roki Sasaki, Round 7, Pick 96 (Sugar Land Skeeters)** — The Japanese phenom who throws 102 mph with a splitter from hell, picked in Round 7. Yes, there are innings concerns. Yes, the Dodgers will be careful. But even 120 innings of Sasaki-level dominance is worth a Round 7 pick. The Skeeters did their homework.

3. **Luis Robert Jr., Round 11, Pick 141 (North Correa)** — Here's a guy who, when healthy, hits .280 with 30-homer power and plus speed. Yes, 'when healthy' is doing a lot of work. But at pick 141? This is free upside. If Robert plays 130 games, this pick looks like genius.

**THE 3 WORST REACHES:**

1. **Mike Trout, Round 6, Pick 75 (General Hospital)** — I love Mike Trout. You love Mike Trout. We all have the 2012-2019 version of Mike Trout tattooed on our hearts. But that player doesn't exist anymore. He's played 130 games TOTAL in the last four seasons. Picking him at 75 is drafting a memory.

2. **Jacob deGrom, Round 5, Pick 65 (Buttville Skidmarks)** — DeGrom in a contract year! DeGrom healthy! DeGrom... who has thrown 74 innings in two years. This is the fantasy equivalent of getting back together with your ex because 'they've changed.' They haven't.

3. **Emmanuel Clase, Round 4, Pick 52 (BaseballPro77)** — Clase is elite. 43 saves, 0.61 ERA, unhittable cutter. But Round 4 for a reliever in a 12-category league where you can find RP value late? This feels like a full round too early.

**V. Five Bold Predictions for 2026**

1. **Riverside Roughnecks will finish top 3** — The Henderson steal, plus Chourio, Machado, and Albies? This is the roster that breaks their 9th-9th-11th curse. Book it.

2. **North Correa will finish in the top half for the first time** — Bobby Witt Jr. alone wins them categories. If Luis Robert and Spencer Strider provide anything, they'll escape the basement.

3. **Sugar Land Skeeters will NOT repeat as champions** — Back-to-back is the hardest thing in fantasy sports. The Sasaki pick is great, but I'm fading the Tatis injury risk.

4. **BaseballPro77's decline continues to 9th or worse** — The Clase pick, the Bichette gamble, the overall lack of pitching depth. This former champion is cooked.

5. **Corbin Carroll will return to his 2023 form** — The Wombats are betting on it, and I think they're right. Carroll at pick 13 will look like a steal by June.

**VI. The Pick: Champion Crowned**

This is where I'm supposed to play it safe, pick the defending champion, and hedge my bets. I'm not going to do that.

**The 2026 MillerLite® BeerLeagueBaseball champion will be the Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs.**

Here's the case: They've finished 4th, 5th, and 2nd the last three years. They know how to build a roster. **Vladimir Guerrero Jr.** is entering his prime. **Bryce Harper** is still a top-15 bat. **Corbin Burnes** and **Framber Valdez** give them two horses. **Ronald Acuña Jr.** at pick 64 is the kind of high-upside gamble that wins championships — if he's healthy, he's MVP-caliber. **George Kirby** at 133 is the late-round SP you need. **Seth Lugo** and **Tanner Houck** provide depth.

This is a team built for the 12-category format. They can win hitting categories AND pitching categories. They have depth at every position. They've been the bridesmaid three straight years.

This is the year the Wonderdogs finally win something.

(And if I'm wrong, I'll be here next March blaming the Acuña pick. That's the beauty of this thing. We do it all again next year.)
